title: Chiari Malformation: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Chiari Malformation
Identify key diagnostic features of Chiari Malformation
Apply clinical reasoning to Chiari Malformation management
Recognize common pitfalls in Chiari Malformation

sections.title: Overview of Chiari Malformation
sections.content:
**Chiari Malformation**



**Key Points:**
• Type I: cerebellar tonsils herniate >5mm below foramen magnum; often presents in young adults
• Symptoms: occipital headache worse with Valsalva/cough, neck pain, syringomyelia (60-70%)
• Type II (Arnold-Chiari): cerebellar vermis + brainstem herniation, ALWAYS associated with myelomeningocele

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Type I: cerebellar tonsils herniate >5mm below foramen magnum; often presents in young adults
• Symptoms: occipital headache worse with Valsalva/cough, neck pain, syringomyelia (60-70%)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Type II (Arnold-Chiari): cerebellar vermis + brainstem herniation, ALWAYS associated with myelomeningocele
• Type III: rare, cerebellar herniation into occipital/cervical encephalocele
• Treatment Type I: posterior fossa decompression if symptomatic; Type II: address myelomeningocele + hydrocephalus

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Chiari Malformation:**

• Type II (Arnold-Chiari): cerebellar vermis + brainstem herniation, ALWAYS associated with myelomeningocele
• Type III: rare, cerebellar herniation into occipital/cervical encephalocele
• Treatment Type I: posterior fossa decompression if symptomatic; Type II: address myelomeningocele + hydrocephalus

options.explanation: Treatment Type I: posterior fossa decompression if symptomatic; Type II: address myelomeningocele + hydrocephalus

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases
